item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like everyone else here, I really like the way you described this SCP. The way you use infohazards leaves a haunting yet great impression, and the concept itself is fun to think about.

The only issue I have is that simply stating that Mikey has autism is a bit strange, like saying he’s got a colour on his shirt. Adding which type of autism Mikey has would be beneficial, as it would paint a better mental picture of him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more elaborate a lie, the more likely it will be detected.

As it stands, the powers that be can just say "you aren't cleared to know what he can do". Leaving the staff with all sorts of questions and zero reason to doubt Mikey is the only important thing in the room.
If the cover is embellished with telekinesis, or any other particulars, then there is the chance someone on the watch shift may start wondering "why don't I ever see this guy doing that thing from the files?"
From there they could start questioning other aspects of the documentation, and it's just a short hop to paying some fleeting attention to the bear, wondering why Bugsy is important enough to get a mention by name in the file, and the whole Akira scenario gets rolling again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kinda wish that the cover-up description said more about what makes Mikey anomalous. Sure, a later document says that any independent movement by Bugsy could be chalked up to being an effect of Mikey's anomaly, but nobody is ever told what Mikey can do. All we get is "Don't ever take away his bear", but no explanation of what would happen if Bugsy was taken from Mikey. At least just give him telekinetic powers or something. Explains why the bear moves to low level researchers and also explains why they shouldn't take it from him. Don't want to make a telekinetic angry, after all.

With that being said, this is still well done, so its a +1 from me (even if I wouldn't buy Mikey being the real anomaly when reading the low-clearance version).

EDIT: a word

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's it with you and US Customary Measurement Units? It's almost as if you scour the entire site for non-metric measurement just so you could crack down on them, all the while ignoring Akiva, Hume, and EVE that's entirely imaginary and measures things that doesn't even exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"6 foot 4 inches tall and 280 pounds"… I know the author wanted to make a Skip that breaks rules of writing Skips; and the article was excellent, very well done; but I hate Imperial measures too much to be able to upvote it. Only three countries use Imperial, metric is used everywhere else. USE METRIC!
No vote. I wish I could upvote, but you used Imperial measures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i want to like this scip. i want to give it the upvote it so clearly deserves. it's well-written, engaging, and terrifying. the only problem? the cover-up scip isn't anomalous. he's just a mentally disabled man with an intense attachment to a teddy bear; that sounds perfectly normal to me. i kinda doubt most Foundation employees would be perfectly satisfied with that explanation, and that almost single-handedly ruins this for me. then again, i can't think of anything better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Novote. It is a nice SCP on several aspects, but one. As a mentally disabled person myself, I'm not sure if I like how this skip is contained, much less how they're think to do it.

I'm autistic, and I can say that if one of us got obsessed with this thing, it would be a matter of few days, or mere hours, for this "mentally challenged" D-Class to become the single greatest specialist on this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though I understand the reasoning, it seems out of place on the measure system. Metric is pretty much how I think about someone's measurements. Like how I'm 1,69 meter tall and weight 92 kilograms (I need to loss a lot of weight, by the way). Pounds and Feet don't look to me like measures for a person, it seems just medieval.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's used to describe a person's measurements:

Yes, including a weight in pounds rather than stone. Since Michael isn't dry goods, this indicates that these are US Customary rather than Imperial measures. Regardless, this isn't SI.

Shouldn't this be:

Yes, unless there's some subtle meta-reason for the US Customary units here that I am missing. Have you tried PM'ing the author? I believe they are still active.

Edit: According to this post: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/t-455543/scp-1055#post-1409078
It's intentional, though I can't say that I agree with the reasoning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's used to describe a person's measurements:

He is a Caucasian male, 6 foot 4 inches tall and 280 pounds,

Shouldn't this be:

He is a Caucasian male, 1.9 meters tall and 127 kilograms,

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It isn't, people aren't dry goods. Though it shouldn't be in US customary either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fantastic scip. +1 One question though: why the imperial measurements? Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is adorable. +1
Containing an infohazard bear with hugs, take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even if it's behavior was harmless, you still have to worry about it reaching critical mass and turning the planet into a star or worse…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
He would just become calm and non-violent?

A non-violent 682 seems like a good idea; but do you really wanna have a 682 around that is incapable of fear?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I once read a short story about an automated, solar-powered tank that would only attack you if you were afraid of it, and was able to evade almost all danger. The tank was being tested on a Pacific island, the natives of which had been temporarily evacuated a la Bikini Atoll. Someone became afraid of the tank for no real reason, so it began killing, which led to infectious fear, which led to more deaths. Eventually, everyone on the island was killed. Then a hurricane cleared away all the resulting carnage, leaving only the tank as evidence. The natives then returned and rebuilt, no idea what the tank was for or capable of. This SCP reminded me of that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Keep the end of the world at bay with hugs"

I just love that sentence.

Upvoted.

item.title: Site Inconsistency
item.content:encoded: I don't know why nobody has noticed this yet but I have just seen an inconsistency about what site 1055 is located at. It says in the article for it that 1055 is reached from Site-77, but on the page Secure Facilities Locations, it says it is at Site-76.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All this Keter needs is a hug, apparently. Upvoted. : )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice twist. I tend to like infohazards due to the mindscrew factor. I also like how amnesiacs do nothing. Because the foundation has amnesiacs readily available, my standard reaction to most infohazards is "So take 2 Class A's and call you in the morning?", which kind of spoils the drama. But I got to "amnesiacs of any class are not sufficient to eliminate awareness" and is was an "Oh, sh-" moment.

item.title: As stated above
item.content:encoded:
OH GOD NO!
I really like this SCP and the concept of a Class D holding the fate of the world in his hands (literally) is very amusing yet terrifying

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: I'm probably alone on this, but having a Keter-level infohazard upvoted this high adds to the horror.
Still a tad underrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I know about double-blind testing, but I was wondering whether there was a clearer connection I was missing. You're probably right about the limited information thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Double-blind" here is an analogy to double-blind testing. What exactly does it mean in that context is unclear. At a guess, each of the groups where told a different version of what was happening and what 1055 could do, all groups knew that (without knowing other groups' versions), but none of them knew which version is true. This way, they fear less, because the whole situation is a bit unreal, and so they don't cause Bugsy to grow as much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What are "Double-blind recovery teams"? (From the third additional information)
I could understand blind recovery teams, but I'm at a loss to understand what double-blind means here. Can anyone explain?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saving the world with Hugs. I absolutely love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Weird - a mentally challenged dude who saves the world by hugging an abnormally-realistic teddy bear (that is a bear - how does nobody notice that) - but I like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it was a joke… because of 055 being an anti-meme and all

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you seen my bear Tibbers?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-055
Many times, folks refer to an SCP as just the number (especially 682, 173, 343) so if you see a number just floating about all lonely like a proper name, that's the most likely case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the disagreement between the 05. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: wtf is 055?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OH GOD NO!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have an idea.

Perceive it as Winnie the Pooh.

Only its SIZE grows as more people know about it; its behavior is entirely determined by WHAT people think of it. When they think it's dangerous, it will be. If they think it's safe, it will be.

Seems like as long as you can convince yourself it's harmless, you're good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When in the boy scouts, I once got to camp out in a military base and get a tour of a decommissioned nuclear bunker, where the launch codes would be keyed in. According to the guide, if the people on site ever got the orders to launch, they would be given encrypted coordinates to input, and would have no idea where the missiles would be heading.

Of course, if we tried to use a similar system to destroy 1055, it would probably go Fubar somewhere along the line. Someone as to create the encrypted coordinates, after all.
And a happy +100 to you Sorts! Congrats man!! You are now also part of the exclusive "More than 1 +100 club."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, I think that was scp-048, which became the Cursed SCP number.

On a side note, I wonder how old a thread has to be before it's considered thread necro?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't we have a similar problem with 055, leading to the forgetfulness thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
well shit

I didn't even know about infohazard until I read this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah! So you did end up making your own heartwarming keter teddy bear! Well done sir. Going from "WTF this is a classic self insert" to "OHHHH you clever bastard" was pretty memorable. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the consensus is that tags are fully out of character. Hence why SCP-1500 is tagged the way it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wondering if we should remove the "ursine" tag, because that spoils the revelation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well done. O5 commentary seems unnecessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everyone's already said anything I could say. It hits all the rules with a hammer and then hits ignorance is bliss and oh god.

Upvoted with tears in my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh god oh god oh god you just made it ten times worse with that explanation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
those countries don't matter

;) It is actually a stylistic choice on my part. Metric is neat and orderly and perfectly scientific, and I want Mikey to be described by other means, just like the description specifically tells people to refer to him by name and with normal pronouns. You're not supposed to see him the way everything else on the wiki is described.

I wasn't quite aiming for breaking so many of the rules with this one, I was just trying to build backwards from hugs as a containment procedure. Somehow that turned into an orgy of everything you shouldn't do when writing an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted, translated and upvoted again. Great example on how to write an object that violates the Cliche list guidelines yet manages to stay awesome.
On an unrelated note - I want to see 682 adapt to THAT!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: OH. That Zach. He's a good guy, I remember he was my best friend in grade school.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: scp-1500 Here,Sorts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not familiar with Zach Callahan, and searching it just gives me names of people named Zach Callahan, was that a movie or book or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Behold the Bearscape.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I noticed this was pretty similar to Zach Callahan. Still, it's quite well executed (Zach Callahan was a bit of a rush job), and definitely different enough to stand on its own. I quite like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very neat. Like a much neater version of Zachary Callahan, which is neat on itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless the reader is from a country that uses metric =^.^=

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely fantastic. A new favorite of mine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think this thing would be that hard to decommision.

Just get someone with expertise in memetic stuff. He should be good with like, checking his toughts and keeping calm and all, so as to not startle the thing. Chuck him into the chamber with it.

Maybe give him a sidearm to. Have him calmly shoot it. Should work. I mean, what's the worst that could happen?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I read most of the way through and though "this is well written and an excellent concept. I don't see anything wrong at all here."

Then I read the O5 banter at the end and thought "This is absolutely horrible, and O5-7 is the worst person ever."

I like it when writers remind me of the horrible things that the Foundation does for the sake of containment, often without question just because it works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hostile action makes it bigger and meaner. The more violent the action, the bigger and meaner it grows; thus, it outgrows any attempts at defeating it violently. You could punch a chunk out of its chest with a shotgun - but the moment you pulled the trigger, its mass (and temper) increased by an amount greater than the mass you were removing with the weapon. Somehow, this even applies to remotely-detonated explosives or automated guns.

It's like trying to beat up the Ang Lee version of the Hulk. Hit it with a Tomahawk missile, and it's suddenly bigger than an Abrams and meaner than my ex-wife once a month.
